title: Quick Prep: Minced Herbs for a One‑Pan Greek Chicken Skillet
keyword: herb scissors Greek chicken skillet recipe
meta_description: Make this vibrant one-pan meal fast! Discover our herb scissors Greek chicken skillet recipe for quick prep and fresh, zesty flavor without the chopping hassle.
html:
Nothing beats the bright, zesty pop of a Mediterranean meal on a busy Tuesday evening, but the prep work can often kill the momentum. When I set out to perfect a quick weeknight dinner, I wanted something that felt gourmet but didn't leave me with a cutting board full of tiny leaves to chop for twenty minutes. That is exactly why I developed this herb scissors Greek chicken skillet recipe. It combines the heartiness of chicken and vegetables with the fresh kick of parsley and dill, utilizing a tool that cuts the chopping time down to seconds.

Why Your Knife Needs a Night Off

We have all been there: you have a hot pan ready to go, but you are still stuck trying to finely mince a mountain of fresh parsley with a chef's knife. The leaves stick to the blade, they slide off the board, and it is frankly tedious. This is where switching your method changes the game.

For this dish, fresh herbs aren't just a garnish; they are an essential component that cuts through the richness of the olives and feta. To make this feasible on a weeknight, I reach for my Herb Scissors. These five-blade herb shears are designed specifically to mince cilantro, chives, and basil in one snip. Instead of rocking a knife back and forth, you simply snip the herbs directly over the pan or a bowl. It is incredibly satisfying and creates a uniform, fluffy cut that distributes flavor perfectly.

The Mise en Place for Your Herb Scissors Greek Chicken Skillet Recipe

Because this is a one-pan meal, the actual cooking process moves quickly. You want to have everything ready before the chicken hits the cast iron. The beauty of this recipe is that the hardest vegetable prep is slicing a lemon and chopping a red onion, which takes far less time than mincing herbs by hand.

  1. Chicken: 1.5 lbs boneless, skinless chicken thighs (cut into bite-sized pieces).
  2. Veggies: 1 large red onion (sliced into thin wedges), 1 pint cherry tomatoes (halved), 1 cup Kalamata olives (pitted).
  3. Aromatics: 4 cloves garlic (minced), 1 lemon (sliced into rounds, plus juice for finishing).
  4. Seasoning: 2 tsp dried oregano, 1 tsp smoked paprika, salt, pepper, and olive oil.
  5. The Fresh Finish: A generous bunch of fresh flat-leaf parsley and dill.
  6. Dairy: 1/2 cup crumbled feta cheese.

Cooking the Skillet Step-by-Step

Start by heating a tablespoon of olive oil in a large, deep skillet over medium-high heat. Pat the chicken pieces dry and season them generously with salt, pepper, and the smoked paprika. Sear the chicken in the hot pan until golden brown on all sides. You don't need to cook it all the way through yet, just get a nice crust. Remove the chicken and set it aside on a plate.

In the same pan, add the sliced red onion. Scrape up the browned bits from the bottom of the pan—that’s where the flavor lives. Cook the onion for about 3 minutes until it softens, then add the garlic and dried oregano. Stir for just a minute until fragrant.

Next, add the cherry tomatoes and olives. Let this mixture cook for another 3-4 minutes. The tomatoes will start to blister and release their juices, creating a light sauce base. Pour in the lemon slices and return the chicken (and any accumulated juices) to the pan. Reduce the heat to medium-low, cover, and simmer for about 10 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and tender.

The Final Snip: Adding Fresh Herbs

This is the step where your Herb Scissors truly shine. Dried oregano provides the earthy base note, but fresh parsley and dill provide the high notes that make this dish taste like a Greek island vacation. Usually, I would dread chopping two different types of fine herbs at the end of a meal, but the shears make it effortless.

Take your washed and dried bunches of parsley and dill. Hold them over the skillet and use the five-blade shears to snip them directly onto the chicken. Because the shears have five blades, one pass is equivalent to five knife chops. You get a confetti of fresh greenery instantly. Toss the pan gently to wilt the herbs slightly and coat everything in those fresh juices. Squeeze the juice of half a lemon over the skillet for acidity.

Serving and Cleanup

Serve the skillet hot, straight from the stove if you want a rustic table presentation, or plate it individually with a hefty sprinkle of feta cheese on top. The salty, creamy cheese pairs perfectly with the bright, snipped herbs.

Now, for the best part: cleanup. If you have ever tried to wash a chef's knife after cutting sticky herbs like dill or fresh chives, you know the leaves can cling to the blade. The Herb Scissors solve this too because they come with a cleaning comb. Once you are done, you just run the comb between the blades to push out any stuck bits of green. A quick hand wash or a cycle in the dishwasher (if they are dishwasher safe, though hand washing is usually better for blade sharpness) and you are done.

FAQ

Can I use dried herbs instead of fresh in this recipe?

While you can substitute dried herbs, the flavor profile will change significantly. The fresh parsley and dill add a bright, grassy freshness that dried herbs lack. If you must use dried, reduce the amount by half and add them at the beginning with the oregano, but I highly recommend using fresh herbs and the shears for the authentic taste.

How do I clean sticky residue off the herb scissors?

The easiest way is to use the included cleaning comb. Simply run the teeth of the comb through the spaces between the five blades to push out any clinging herbs. If you don't have the comb handy, soaking them in warm soapy water for a few minutes and using a vegetable brush works well too.

What other proteins work in this skillet?

This recipe is very versatile. While chicken thighs remain juicy and tender, you can easily swap them for boneless chicken breasts (watch the cook time so they don't dry out), shrimp (add them in the last 3-4 minutes of cooking), or even white fish fillets like cod or halibut.
